questions.text: I maintain a clean inbox and respond to messages within a consistent timeframe.
I find that I miss the political dimensions of workplace conversations.
I hold the view that my personal time is too valuable to spend on obligations that do not advance my vision.
I think that the capacity for objective assessment in the presence of human pain is a form of strength that serves the common good.
I feel uncomfortable when someone challenges my authority in front of the group.
I find that I do not read the social meaning into someone's choice of clothing for a particular occasion.
I find that I navigate emotionally complex situations more effectively by treating them as intellectual problems with emotional dimensions, rather than the reverse.
I maintain organized systems for managing my files, finances, and personal records.
I tend to let the group decide when the meeting wraps and what the next steps are.
I believe that the moral demand of suffering is clearest when it calls for action that is inconvenient, costly, or socially awkward.
I tend to re-feel past emotional experiences at nearly their original intensity when I remember them.
I hold the view that the sincerity of my moral commitments is most accurately measured by how I respond to suffering, not by what I say about it.
I often forgo recognition so that others can be acknowledged instead.
I hold the view that the best thing I can do is become the strongest version of myself.
I feel that maintaining systems takes energy away from the creative work I'd rather do.
I notice that I can attend to multiple tasks even when something emotional is unresolved.
I prefer to pitch my idea and get others on board with it.
I believe that staying loose and responsive compounds into a richer range of experiences.
I label storage containers, drawers, and shelves so everything has a designated place.
I feel that the moral pull I experience in the presence of suffering is evidence of a well-functioning conscience.
I notice when a person mirrors another person's gestures as a sign of rapport.
I think that the ability to evaluate suffering without being consumed by it is what distinguishes sustainable helpers from those who burn out.
I feel that my personal ambitions deserve dedicated time and resources.
I believe that mastery comes from continually pushing into unfamiliar territory.
When I'm emotionally activated, my peripheral vision narrows and everything feels urgent.
I feel confident making decisions on behalf of a group.
I am attuned to the difference between genuine interest and polite listening.
I experience day-to-day life at a comfortable, sustainable emotional volume.
I find that a vivid dream can leave me emotionally shaken for an entire morning.
I believe key discussions should be open to anyone who wants to participate.
I welcome challenges to my suggestions because they strengthen the group's thinking.
I hold the view that real strength is the willingness to put yourself last.
My emotions feel like background music — always present, but never drowning out the rest of my experience.
I can tell from the way two people greet each other how long it has been since they last spoke and how that gap felt.
I often do not notice when someone changes the subject to avoid discomfort.
I feel that if I have the ability to ease someone's burden, hesitation is unacceptable.
